entry.title: SAP DMS Client – A Desktop Companion for Cloud Integration Data Archiving
entry.content:
Why I Built This

While looking into Cloud Integration data archiving, I came across Kishor Gopinathan's blog post SAP Cloud Integration – Data Archiving to BTP SDM. It walks through the full setup — creating service instances, building the CloudIntegration_LogArchive destination, and activating archiving via API.

I followed it while onboarding onto a new Integration Suite landscape, and it worked great. But I noticed that the workflow assumes we have a REST client like Postman at hand to call APIs, and some familiarity with CMIS to browse the archived content. Not everyone on the team has that setup ready — and honestly, assembling JSON payloads and fetching OAuth tokens just to activate archiving felt like unnecessary friction for what should be a straightforward task.

So I built a small desktop app to lower that barrier: the SAP DMS Client. I built it with the help of AI tooling, which made it realistic to go from idea to working desktop app within my onboarding timeline. It reads our DMS and Cloud Integration service keys and gives us a UI for managing repositories, exporting destination files, activating archiving, and browsing archived content — no REST client or CMIS knowledge required. The code is open source, so anyone can inspect and adapt it.

What It Does

The app is built with Electron, React, and Material UI (following SAP Horizon design guidelines). Once we load our two service key files — one for DMS, one for Process Integration Runtime — we get four things:

  • Repository management — create, list, and delete DMS repositories without CMIS API calls
  • Destination export — generate a CloudIntegration_LogArchive.json with all credentials pre-filled, ready to import into BTP Cockpit
  • Archiving activation — activate Cloud Integration data archiving in one click (the app handles the OAuth2 token exchange)
  • File explorer — browse the YYYY/MM/DD folder structure that Cloud Integration creates in the repository

The destination export is probably the biggest time saver. Instead of manually extracting clientId, clientSecret, tokenServiceURL, and the SDM endpoint from the service key JSON, the app assembles the full destination file automatically. We just import it into BTP Cockpit and move on.

Here's what the generated file looks like:

{
  "Name": "CloudIntegration_LogArchive",
  "Type": "HTTP",
  "URL": "https://<sdm-endpoint>/browser",
  "Authentication": "OAuth2ClientCredentials",
  "RepositoryId": "<repository-id>",
  "clientId": "<from-service-key>",
  "clientSecret": "<from-service-key>",
  "tokenServiceURL": "https://<auth-endpoint>/oauth/token"
}

No copy-paste errors, no missed fields.

One thing to note: when we import the destination into BTP Cockpit, all fields are populated automatically except the client secret — BTP doesn't include it in the import format for security reasons. We'll need to grab the clientSecret from our DMS service key file and paste it manually into the destination configuration in the Cockpit after import.

What We Need Before Starting

The same BTP services from the original blog:

Service Plan What We Need

Integration Suiteenterprise / standardCloud Integration capability activated
Process Integration RuntimeapiService instance + service key
Document Management Service, Integration OptionstandardService instance + service key

Setup details: Initial Setup for DMS and Creating Service Key for Cloud Integration.

The app is on GitHub at github.com/sap-ef/da-dms-client. The easiest way to get started is to download a pre-built installer from the Releases page:

  • macOS — .dmg file
  • Windows — .exe installer

Alternatively, we can build from source with Node.js 18+:

git clone https://github.com/sap-ef/da-dms-client.git
cd da-dms-client
npm install
npm run electron:dev

The Workflow in Practice

Once the app is running, the flow follows a specific order — each step depends on the previous one:

  1. Load the DMS service key in Settings — this connects us to our Document Management Service instance
  2. Create a repository — we need a repository before we can generate a destination (the destination includes the repository ID)
  3. Export the destination file — the app generates the CloudIntegration_LogArchive.json with all the values from the service key
  4. Import the destination into the Integration Suite subaccount — in BTP Cockpit under Connectivity > Destinations (remember to fill in the client secret manually after import)
  5. Load the Cloud Integration service key in Settings — this connects us to the Process Integration Runtime instance
  6. Activate data archiving — this only works if the destination already exists in BTP, otherwise Cloud Integration won't know where to write the logs

 

Don't expect logs to appear immediately. As described in the original blog, the log collection process runs on a 7-day cycle — so it may take several days before the first archived files show up in the repository.

After archiving is active, the File Explorer lets us browse what Cloud Integration has stored. The folder structure is date-based (2026/04/15/) with .zip archives of message processing logs for each day.

Conclusion

Kishor Gopinathan's original blog remains the foundation for understanding Cloud Integration data archiving to SDM. The DMS Client just makes the repetitive parts easier so we can spend less time on plumbing and more on the integration itself.

The project is open source at github.com/sap-ef/da-dms-client. If you've dealt with Cloud Integration archiving in your landscape, I'd be curious to hear how you've approached it — or if there are other parts of the DMS workflow that could use better tooling. Drop a comment or open an issue.

Links

  • SAP DMS Client — GitHub
  • SAP Cloud Integration – Data Archiving to BTP SDM — Kishor Gopinathan
  • Initial Setup for Document Management Service
  • Creating Service Instance and Service Key (Cloud Integration)

entry.title: Step-by-Step Guide to Fine-Grained Access Control in SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh
entry.content:
Introduction

When working with SAP Integration Suite Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) across multiple brokers, it is essential to enforce strict user access controls. This ensures adherence to the separation of concerns principle and protects provisioned brokers from unauthorized actions. Without appropriate controls, users may inadvertently disrupt services—such as by deleting a broker or spinning up new brokers—which can compromise the environment or affect other users.

While AEM offers standard inbuilt roles designed for granular access management, these roles often do not align with the specific combinations required for specific landscape setup. For example, you may want users to have the ability to manage queues (create, edit, delete) within a specific broker, but restrict their ability to perform any other critical actions elsewhere. Achieving this level of intermediate access control is possible with Group management feature of AEM in conjunction with external Identity Provider (IdP) configurations.

To learn how to set up the Cloud Identity Service, refer to the blog post: “Streamlining User Management in Advanced Event Mesh with SAP CIS and Identity Federation.”

This blog post, co-authored by Sunny Kapoor and Tobias Griebe outlines the necessary steps to set up and enforce such granular access, ensuring users have only the permissions needed for their designated tasks—thereby protecting your AEM environment from accidental disruptions.

Why User Groups over Users in User Management of AEM?

While AEM offers standard inbuilt roles, these typically grant either full access to a feature set or restrict users to viewer-only permissions. In scenarios where controlled, intermediate access is required—such as delegating limited management of a specific event broker—standard roles may not suffice. This is where the “Mission Control User” role becomes essential and is available only if you have user groups configured. Mission Control Users have limited viewing access in Cluster Manager. They can't see event broker services they are not assigned permissions to, and they have no access to Mesh Manager. Users can be given greater access to event broker services by assigning permissions to them. For more information about event broker service-specific access levels, see Configuring User Access to Event Broker Services.

Prerequisites

Before you enable group and role mapping for your advanced event mesh account, you need to do the following:Configure your SAP Cloud Identity Service instance to provide claim values that you can map to user groups. For more information, see the SAP Cloud Identity Services documentation.

Also, you can refer to the blog post: “Streamlining User Management in Advanced Event Mesh with SAP CIS and Identity Federation.”

Creating a User Group

In this example configuration, we have created two User Groups by following the official documentation.First user group called Administrators where we have assigned two roles – Account Administrator and Insights Advanced Editor.

Second User Group called Participants where we have assigned single role i.e. Mission Control Users that can access Cluster Manager but have limited access and viewing capabilities.

Further event broker service access levels i.e. Viewer, Editor or Manager needs to be granted to user groups with the Mission Control User as per the requirement. In this example, we will assign Editor user access to one of the event brokers so that users with Participants group assignment can manage queues (create, edit, delete) within a specific broker, but restrict their ability to perform any other critical actions.

Group Management

You can assign roles to user groups rather than directly to individual users. By mapping claims received from your identity provider (IdP) to these user groups, users can be automatically assigned to the appropriate groups upon authentication. When group management is enabled, users are automatically added to groups based on the claim mapping that you have configured. For example, if your IdP returns a claim values such as “service_manager”, users with that claim value would be automatically assigned to the user group that the claim value is mapped to. If no claim returned for the user is mapped to a group when a user authenticates, the user can be added to a default role or denied access.

After you configure group management you can continue to invite new users manually or you can enable just-in-time provisioning to add new users to groups based on the existing claim mapping configuration when a user successfully authenticates using SSO.

To set up group management for your account, see Configuring Group Management.

In this example we do click on the “Group Management” button in the user groups screen and do the configuration as per the screenshot to enable it. It mainly have the mapping of claims received from your IdP to the user groups created earlier.

  1. This is the attribute key sent from the IdP to AEM.
  2. Groups are configured in the IdP, and users are added to these groups within the IdP.

Configure user access in the Cluster Manager

Last step is to choose the specific event broker for which you like to further control the access like in this example we will assign Editor user access to one of the event brokers so that users with Participants group assignment can manage queues (create, edit, delete) within a specific broker, but restrict their ability to perform any other critical actions.

In Cluster Manager, select the Event Broker, click the three dots, and choose Set User Access.

Click Add User Groups, select the Participants group, and set access to Editor and finally Save.

Important Points to Consider

  1. Advanced event mesh also lets you enable just-in-time provisioning, which lets you onboard new advanced event mesh users without inviting them manually. If you don't select this option, you'll need to manually invite each user in AEM. Note that the Invite button in the Users tab is disabled when group management is enabled, so you'll have to disable group management every time you want to invite users.
  2. Do not select Customize Default User Group and select a user group from the Default User Group drop-down list that has the least privileges without proper testing. When selected, if no defined claim mappings match the user claim value, the user is added to the specified group. This could unintentionally lower privileges for all users, including administrators, which might make it impossible to change user management settings and could lead to the tenant becoming unusable. When not selected, if no claim value for the user is mapped to a user group, the user is denied access to advanced event mesh.
  3. Make sure you test the setup by Clicking Test Access > Run Test to test the updates to ensure that your Account Administrator account is allowed access with the new settings. A message confirms whether you will still have Account Administrator privileges on the account after the settings are applied. If you will lose Account Administrator access, update the settings before continuing.

Conclusion

With this configuration, when a non-admin user logs in, they will only have visibility of the specific event broker to which access has been granted. They will be able to manage queues (create, edit, delete) within that broker, while their ability to perform other critical actions—such as creating or deleting an event broker—will remain restricted.

entry.title: May 2026 Developer Challenge - From events to intelligence: Building an event-driven RAG pipeline
entry.content:
This month's developer challenge is a bit different from previous ones. Instead of focusing exclusively on SAP technologies, the goal is for you to learn the concepts, architect a solution and implement it using whatever tools you have access to — SAP, open-source, trial, paid products, use your favourite programming language or whatever agent you fancy using. At the end of each week's challenge, I will share how I solved it, but the path you take to get there is yours to choose.

Here is what we will build over the four weeks: An event-driven integration pipeline that consumes events from a broker, vectorizes the data within those event payloads, stores the vectors in a vector store, and makes them available for Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG).

The screenshot below can give you an idea of what a RAG pipeline can look like. If you want to learn more about how the integration flow below was built - check this out.

This is a challenge that sits at the intersection of two worlds that are increasingly converging: event-driven architectures and Generative AI. Understanding how to feed real-time, event-driven data into an AI pipeline is becoming a foundational skill — and that's exactly what we're going to build.

Links to each week will be added here as they are published:

  • Week 1: Getting familiar with the events (4th of May)
  • Week 2: Connecting to the broker and consuming events  (11th of May)
  • Week 3: Vectorizing the event payload (To be published  18th of May)
  • Week 4: Storing vectors and enabling RAG (To be published  25th of May)

entry.title: Consuming and Upgrading Adapters in SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
• Overview
  • Consuming an Adapterin SAP Cloud Integration Suite
  • 1. Adapter Installation by Creating a New Integration Flow
  • 2. Copy the Adapter from Discover tab:
  • 3. Upgrading an Existing Adapter
  • Conclusion

Overview 

SAP Cloud Integration (SAP CI) provides a wide range of adapters to connect with external systems, cloud applications, and on-premises landscapes. While many standard adapters are readily available in SAP Integration Suite, Discover tab in SAP Cloud Integration allows you to consume adapter upgrades and latest application adapters available on SAP Integration Suite. 

This blog explains how we consume an adapter from SAP Business Accelerator Hub and how to upgrade an existing adapter version in SAP Cloud Integration. 

Consuming an Adapter in SAP Cloud Integration Suite 

1. Adapter Installation by Creating a New Integration Flow

Before proceeding, it is worth noting that there is no manual installation option for the any Adapter. The steps below explain how to consume it from the Discover workspace to the Design workspace in the SAP Integration tenant. 

1. Navigate to the Design tab

2.Open the required integration package > Click Edit

3.In the Artifacts tab, select Add > Integration Flow > Create an integration flow and give a name.

4. Proceed to the newly created integration flow and click on Edit > select End > Drag pointer to Receiver.

5. Select the Adapter from the list. After selecting,  the adapter is imported into the Design workspace of your SAP Integration Suite tenant. 

    

6. Once the package is imported,  the adapter deployment is automatically triggered. Once the Adapter is deployed, a success message is displayed 

 

2. Copy the Adapter from Discover tab: 

1. Open the Discover Tab and search for your desired adapter like Salesforce / IBM MQ / Google PubSub) and Copy the adapter as shown below. 
Note: This copies the package from the Discover to Design tab.

2. Navigate to the Design tab  and search for the Salesforce Adapter.

3. Click on Artifacts tab and deploy the adapter as shown below: 

4. Navigate to Monitor tab to check the deployment status and verify the adapter version. 

 

3. Upgrading an Existing Adapter

This is the more common hands-on activity. A package from SAP Business Accelerator Hub gets a new version. We may need to update the consumed package in workspace.  

[COMPARE] Check for Updates in Design Tab of SAP Integration Suite 

If you see a package in Design tab with an Update Available option 

Go to: 

  • Design > Search for the existing adapter. 
  • Compare the available version in Discover (Note: This should be the latest available version reflecting SAP Business Accelerator Hub) and current version in your Design workspace.  

[VERIFY] Review Change Log in SAP Business Accelerator Hub 

Before upgrading, always check impact of the change: 

  • New mandatory fields 
  • Support for external APIs 

Best Practice: Never upgrade directly in production without validating the change impact in lower environments first.  

Update the Latest Version into Workspace 

1. Navigate to your Design Tab and search for adapter package which needs to be updated.'

2. Open the adapter package and click on Update Package. 

  

3. Once you click on Update, it automatically triggers  and deploys the new artifact. 

4. Navigate to Monitor tab and verify the adapter version. 

Conclusion

There are many ways to consume an adapter in SAP Cloud Integration.

For new adapters you can 

  • Create a new iflow to select the adapter
  • Copy from Discover Tab

For existing adapters, you can use

  • Update Package

entry.title: CPI iFlow Resource Scanner: How I Built a Simple Web Tool Inside CPI to Find Unused Files
entry.content:
As we work on SAP CPI iFlows over time, we collect extra files. A Groovy script gets added for a new feature, then the feature gets descoped. A Message Mapping gets replaced but the old file stays behind. Soon the resources folder is full of XSD schemas and scripts just sitting there doing nothing.

The problem is that CPI does not show us which files are actually being used. The only option is to click through every step on the canvas to check. This takes too much time, and it needs repeating after every change. Worse, checking the canvas manually misses hidden files - XSD schemas and Groovy UDFs used inside Message Mappings are completely invisible from the canvas view.

This post walks through how I build a resource scanner utility that solves this problem. The tool uses Integration Suite APIs to check the canvas, look inside Message Mappings for hidden dependencies, and report exactly which files are unused. It runs entirely inside CPI and shows results in a browser. The post includes all Groovy scripts, architecture diagrams, and lessons learned from edge cases encountered during development.

1.  Architecture - How It Is Built

The tool is split into two iFlows:

  • UI iFlow: Provides the web page. A GET request returns the HTML interface. A POST request (when you click Scan) forwards to the analysis engine via ProcessDirect.

  • Analysis Engine: Does the actual work of checking files. It only accepts ProcessDirect connections, which means it can be reused by other tools later. This separation has already proven useful.

UI iFlow: GET and POST Request Handling

Analysis Engine: Main Integration Process

2.  The Four Steps of Analysis

Analysis Engine: Local Process Detail

2a.  Step 1: Get the Full List of Files

An OData API call retrieves every file in the iFlow's resource folder:

Endpoint:  https://{baseUrl}/api/v1/IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts
           (Id='{iFlowID}',Version='active')/Resources

The response gets transformed via XSLT to flatten the Atom XML, then a Groovy script sorts files into four lists:

def xml = new XmlSlurper().parseText(message.getBody(String.class))

def groovyCatalog = [], mmapCatalog = [], xsltCatalog = [], xsdCatalog = []

xml.ResourceList.each { item ->
    def name = item.ResourceName?.text()?.trim()
    def type = item.ResourceType?.text()?.trim()
    
    switch (type) {
        case 'groovy': groovyCatalog << name; break
        case 'mmap':   mmapCatalog << name; break
        case 'xslt':   xsltCatalog << name; break
        case 'xsd':    xsdCatalog << name; break
    }
}

message.setProperty("catalog_groovy", groovyCatalog.join(","))
message.setProperty("catalog_mmap",   mmapCatalog.join(","))
message.setProperty("catalog_xslt",   xsltCatalog.join(","))
message.setProperty("catalog_xsd",    xsdCatalog.join(","))

The lists are stored as exchange properties because each subsequent API call overwrites the message body.

2b.  Step 2: Check the Main Canvas

The tool downloads the iFlow ZIP and extracts the .iflw file - the XML representation of the canvas. It searches for files directly attached to steps.

One thing worth noting: the .iflw uses namespaces that vary between CPI versions. Hardcoding prefixes breaks when moving between environments. Using wildcard traversal avoids this:

def xml = new XmlSlurper().parseText(message.getBody(String.class))

def refGroovy = [] as Set, refMmap = [] as Set
def refXslt = [] as Set, refXsd = [] as Set

xml.'**'.findAll { it.name() == 'property' }.each { prop ->
    def key = prop.@key?.toString()
    def val = prop.@value?.toString()

    if (key == 'script' && val.endsWith('.groovy')) 
        refGroovy << val
    
    else if (key == 'mappinguri') {
        def file = val.tokenize('/').last()
        if (file.endsWith('.mmap')) refMmap << file
        else if (file.endsWith('.xsl') || file.endsWith('.xslt')) refXslt << file
    }
    
    else if (key == 'xsd') {
        def file = val.tokenize('/').last()
        if (file.endsWith('.xsd')) refXsd << file
    }
}

message.setProperty("direct_groovy", refGroovy.join(","))
message.setProperty("direct_mmap",   refMmap.join(","))
message.setProperty("direct_xslt",   refXslt.join(","))
message.setProperty("direct_xsd",    refXsd.join(","))

This finds Groovy scripts on Script steps (key='script'), mappings on Mapping steps (key='mappinguri'), and XSDs on XML Validator steps (key='xsd').

2c.  Step 3: Look Inside Message Mappings

This is the step that provides value beyond what manual inspection can do.

Message Mappings hide files inside them. They reference XSD schemas as source and target structures, and Groovy scripts as UDF libraries. None of this appears on the canvas. A schema can look completely unused while being loaded every time a mapping runs.

The tool opens each .mmap file and extracts these hidden references:

  • lnkRole[@role='SOURCE_IFR_MESS'] → source XSD
  • lnkRole[@role='TARGET_IFR_MESS'] → target XSD
  • lnkRole[@role='UsedFuncLib'] → Groovy UDF

A Splitter processes each mapping file. After the Gather step, a script merges the results:

def mappingList = message.getProperty("direct_mmap")?.split(",")?.findAll { it } ?: []
def indirectXsd = [] as Set, indirectGroovy = [] as Set
def deps = [:]

mappingList.each { name ->
    def xsd = message.getProperty("xsd_refs_for_${name}")?.split(",")?.findAll { it } ?: []
    def gvy = message.getProperty("groovy_refs_for_${name}")?.split(",")?.findAll { it } ?: []
    
    indirectXsd.addAll(xsd)
    indirectGroovy.addAll(gvy)
    deps[name] = [xsd: xsd, groovy: gvy]
}

message.setProperty("indirect_xsd", indirectXsd.join(","))
message.setProperty("indirect_groovy", indirectGroovy.join(","))
message.setProperty("mmap_dependencies", new JsonBuilder(deps).toString())


2d.  Step 4: Do the Math

Resource Comparator: Set Arithmetic and Final JSON Assembly

The tool compares the lists. It takes the full catalog from Step 1 and subtracts everything found in Steps 2 and 3:

Unused Groovy = All_Groovy − (Direct_Groovy ∪ Indirect_Groovy_from_Mappings)
Unused MMAP   = All_MMAP − Direct_MMAP
Unused XSLT   = All_XSLT − Direct_XSLT
Unused XSD    = All_XSD − (Direct_XSD_from_Validator ∪ Indirect_XSD_from_Mappings)
def toSet = { prop -> 
    message.getProperty(prop)?.split(",")?.findAll { it }?.toSet() ?: [] as Set 
}

def catG = toSet("catalog_groovy"), catM = toSet("catalog_mmap")
def catX = toSet("catalog_xslt"),   catS = toSet("catalog_xsd")

def dirG = toSet("direct_groovy"),  dirM = toSet("direct_mmap")
def dirX = toSet("direct_xslt"),    dirS = toSet("direct_xsd")
def indS = toSet("indirect_xsd"),   indG = toSet("indirect_groovy")

def usedG = dirG + indG
def usedS = dirS + indS

def output = [
    summary: [
        total:  [groovy: catG.size(), mmap: catM.size(), xslt: catX.size(), xsd: catS.size()],
        used:   [groovy: usedG.size(), mmap: dirM.size(), xslt: dirX.size(), xsd: usedS.size()],
        unused: [groovy: (catG - usedG).size(), mmap: (catM - dirM).size(), 
                 xslt: (catX - dirX).size(), xsd: (catS - usedS).size()]
    ],
    used: [
        direct: [groovy: dirG as List, mmap: dirM as List, xslt: dirX as List, xsd: dirS as List],
        mmap: new JsonSlurper().parseText(message.getProperty("mmap_dependencies") ?: "{}")
    ],
    unused: [groovy: (catG - usedG) as List, mmap: (catM - dirM) as List,
             xslt: (catX - dirX) as List, xsd: (catS - usedS) as List]
]

message.setBody(new JsonBuilder(output).toPrettyString())
message.setHeader("Content-Type", "application/json")

Whatever is left over is an unused file.

3.  Serving HTML from Groovy

If you are building CPI tools, this technique is worth knowing. You do not need an external server to host a web interface.

CPI's HTTPS adapter returns whatever you put in the message body. Set Content-Type: text/html, return an HTML string from Groovy, and the browser renders it as a normal web page:

Message processData(Message message) {
    def httpMethod = message.getHeaders().get("CamelHttpMethod")

    if (httpMethod == "GET") {
        message.setBody(buildPage())
        message.setHeader("Content-Type", "text/html; charset=UTF-8")
        return message
    }
    return message
}

The HTML page includes JavaScript that posts back to the same URL, receives the JSON from the analysis engine, and renders the dashboard. I have reused this pattern for other utilities.

Here is a sample groovy script to serve the WebUI

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message

def Message processData(Message message) {
    
    def httpMethod = message.getHeaders().get("CamelHttpMethod")
    
    if (httpMethod == "GET") {
        message.setBody(buildPage())
        message.setHeader("Content-Type", "text/html; charset=UTF-8")
    }
    // POST path: JSON flows through from ProcessDirect unchanged
    
    return message
}

def String buildPage() {
    return '''<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
<meta charset="UTF-8">
<title>CPI iFlow Resource Scanner</title>
<style>
:root {
  --navy: #0a1628;
  --blue: #0070f2;
  --teal: #0cc4ac;
  --amber: #f59e0b;
  --gray-100: #f2f4f8;
  --gray-600: #68778f;
  --white: #ffffff;
}
* { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; padding: 0; }
body { font-family: system-ui, sans-serif; background: var(--gray-100); }
header { background: linear-gradient(135deg, var(--navy), #1a365d); color: var(--white);
         padding: 16px 24px; display: flex; justify-content: space-between; }
.logo { font-weight: 700; font-size: 16px; }
main { max-width: 1200px; margin: 0 auto; padding: 24px; }
.input-bar { background: var(--white); border-radius: 10px; padding: 16px;
             display: flex; gap: 16px; align-items: flex-end; margin-bottom: 24px; }
.field { display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 6px; flex: 1; }
.field label { font-size: 11px; font-weight: 600; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--gray-600); }
.field input, .field select { padding: 10px; border: 1px solid #ddd; border-radius: 6px; }
.btn { background: var(--blue); color: var(--white); border: none; padding: 10px 24px;
       border-radius: 6px; font-weight: 600; cursor: pointer; }
.btn:disabled { opacity: 0.6; }
.summary-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr); gap: 16px; margin-bottom: 24px; }
.metric-card { background: var(--white); border-radius: 10px; padding: 16px;
               border-top: 3px solid var(--blue); }
.metric-card.unused { border-top-color: var(--amber); }
.metric-title { font-size: 12px; color: var(--gray-600); margin-bottom: 12px; }
.metric-value { font-size: 28px; font-weight: 700; }
.metric-label { font-size: 11px; color: var(--gray-600); }
.panels { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr); gap: 16px; }
.panel { background: var(--white); border-radius: 10px; overflow: hidden; }
.panel-header { padding: 12px 16px; font-weight: 600; border-bottom: 1px solid #eee; }
.panel-body { padding: 12px; }
.resource-item { padding: 6px 8px; font-family: monospace; font-size: 12px; color: #555; }
.resource-item.unused { color: var(--amber); }
#resultsContainer { display: none; }
#resultsContainer.active { display: block; }
</style>
</head>
<body>
<header>
  <div class="logo">CPI iFlow Resource Scanner</div>
  <div style="font-size:12px;opacity:0.7">v1.0</div>
</header>
<main>
  <div class="input-bar">
    <div class="field">
      <label>Tenant</label>
      <select id="tenantSelect"><option>PROD-US</option><option>DEV-US</option></select>
    </div>
    <div class="field" style="flex:2">
      <label>iFlow ID</label>
      <input type="text" id="iflowInput" placeholder="Enter iFlow artifact ID" />
    </div>
    <button class="btn" id="scanBtn" onclick="runScan()">Scan Resources</button>
  </div>
  <div id="resultsContainer"></div>
</main>
<script>
async function runScan() {
  var btn = document.getElementById('scanBtn');
  var container = document.getElementById('resultsContainer');
  btn.disabled = true;
  btn.textContent = 'Scanning...';
  
  try {
    var response = await fetch(window.location.href, {
      method: 'POST',
      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
      body: JSON.stringify({
        tenant: document.getElementById('tenantSelect').value,
        iFlowID: document.getElementById('iflowInput').value
      })
    });
    var data = await response.json();
    container.innerHTML = renderResults(data);
    container.classList.add('active');
  } catch (err) {
    container.innerHTML = '<div style="color:red;padding:20px">Error: ' + err.message + '</div>';
    container.classList.add('active');
  }
  btn.disabled = false;
  btn.textContent = 'Scan Resources';
}

function renderResults(data) {
  var s = data.summary;
  return '<div class="summary-grid">' +
    renderCard('Groovy Scripts', s.total.groovy, s.used.groovy, s.unused.groovy) +
    renderCard('Message Mappings', s.total.mmap, s.used.mmap, s.unused.mmap) +
    renderCard('XSLT Mappings', s.total.xslt, s.used.xslt, s.unused.xslt) +
    renderCard('XSD Schemas', s.total.xsd, s.used.xsd, s.unused.xsd) +
  '</div>' +
  '<div class="panels">' +
    renderPanel('All Resources', data.all, '') +
    renderPanel('Used Resources', data.used.direct, '') +
    renderPanel('Unused Resources', data.unused, 'unused') +
  '</div>';
}

function renderCard(title, total, used, unused) {
  var cls = unused > 0 ? 'metric-card unused' : 'metric-card';
  return '<div class="' + cls + '">' +
    '<div class="metric-title">' + title + '</div>' +
    '<div style="display:flex;gap:20px">' +
      '<div><div class="metric-value">' + total + '</div><div class="metric-label">Total</div></div>' +
      '<div><div class="metric-value">' + used + '</div><div class="metric-label">Used</div></div>' +
      '<div><div class="metric-value">' + unused + '</div><div class="metric-label">Unused</div></div>' +
    '</div></div>';
}

function renderPanel(title, resources, itemClass) {
  var html = '<div class="panel"><div class="panel-header">' + title + '</div><div class="panel-body">';
  ['groovy', 'mmap', 'xslt', 'xsd'].forEach(function(type) {
    var list = resources[type] || [];
    list.forEach(function(name) {
      html += '<div class="resource-item ' + itemClass + '">' + name + '</div>';
    });
  });
  return html + '</div></div>';
}
</script>
</body>
</html>'''
}

4.  What the Dashboard Shows

Full WebUI showing summary metric cards and three-panel result layout

The interface shows:

  • Summary cards with Total / Used / Unused counts per file type
  • Three panels: All Resources, Used Resources, Unused Resources
  • Which XSD schemas and Groovy UDFs are hidden inside each Message Mapping
  • Excel export for documentation

Lessons Learned

While building this tool I encountered a few edge cases which are worth knowing about:

Global Script Bundles. An iFlow can reference Groovy scripts from external Script Collections via the scriptBundleID property. These groovy scripts are not part of the iFlow's own resources folder - they live in a separate, shared package. The scanner will show them as "used" (because they appear in the canvas reference), but they will not appear in the "all" list (because they are not in this iFlow's resources). This is expected behavior, not a bug. If you see a script in "used" that is missing from "all", check whether it comes from a Script Collection.

def isGlobalTarget = parent.children().findAll { it.name() == 'property' }.any { siblingProp ->
    def siblingKey = siblingProp.'**'.find { it.name() == 'key' }?.text()?.trim()
    def siblingValue = siblingProp.'**'.find { it.name() == 'value' }?.text()?.trim()
    
    // Check if the key matches and the value is not null or empty string
    return siblingKey == 'scriptBundleId' && siblingValue
}

Phantom References. Sometimes a Message Mapping internally references a Groovy UDF or XSD that no longer exists in the resources folder. The file may have been deleted, or the mapping was copied from another iFlow without its dependencies. In these cases, Total will not equal Used + Unused - there is a reference to something that is not there. The scanner exposes this inconsistency, which is actually useful: it flags mappings that may fail at runtime.

Commas in Filenames. The scanner stores resource lists as comma-separated strings in exchange properties. If a filename contains a comma (e.g., "Script,Helper,Utils.groovy"), the split logic will treat it as three separate files. This is a delimiter collision. The fix is to avoid commas in filenames - this is good practice anyway since commas are a common delimiter across many tools and systems.

The scanner finds static references - files assigned to steps or declared inside mappings. If a Groovy script constructs a filename at runtime and loads it dynamically, the scanner will not catch that. This is uncommon but worth checking before deleting anything flagged as unused.

Key Takeaways

After reading this post, you should be able to:

  • Serve a browser UI directly from CPI: No external hosting needed. Set Content-Type: text/html and return HTML content from Groovy.
  • Access iFlow internals via OData APIs: The /Resources endpoint gives the full catalog; the ZIP download gives the .iflw canvas file and .mmap mapping files.
  • Detect hidden dependencies: Message Mappings reference XSD schemas and Groovy UDFs internally via lnkRole elements. These are invisible on the canvas but can be extracted by parsing the .mmap XML.
  • Interpret count mismatches: When Total ≠ Used + Unused, it means either an external Script Bundle is in use, or a mapping references a file that no longer exists.
  • Apply this pattern to other tools: The HTML-from-Groovy pattern and the ProcessDirect-based engine separation work for any CPI utility that needs a browser interface.

The JSON output gives a clean list of only the active files. This is useful beyond just cleanup. For example, sending iFlow code to an LLM for documentation works better when you send only the files that are actually in use instead of the entire resource folder. That approach will be covered in the next post.

How do you keep your iFlows clean? Questions, other approaches or edge cases welcome in the comments.

AI Disclosure: AI was used to help structure and refine the language in this post. The technical content, code, and lessons learned are from my own development work.

entry.title: SAP APIM Logging in Splunk
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP API Management often requires centralized transaction logging to support monitoring, troubleshooting, and operational analysis across API flows. While working with SAP APIM, I found it difficult to consistently monitor request and response errors and track client IP addresses, especially when issues had to be investigated across multiple policies and runtime stages. On one implementation, this became a recurring support problem because we could see that requests were failing, but we could not quickly tell whether the failure happened during API key validation, target processing, or response handling. That experience led me to implement a centralized logging pattern with Splunk. In this scenario, the goal was to capture key request and response details, including validation failures, response status, and performance metrics, without introducing significant runtime overhead. This post describes a reusable integration pattern using KVM-based configuration, asynchronous service callout, and PreFlow/PostFlow logging so that structured API logging can be implemented in SAP APIM for improved observability.

Integration Pattern for SAP APIM and Splunk

This approach was chosen to keep the logging design secure, reusable, and lightweight. Splunk endpoint details and authentication values are externalized in KVM so they can be maintained without changing policy code. Logging is separated across PreFlow and PostFlow to capture both early validation failures and end-to-end response details, while asynchronous service callouts help minimize runtime impact on the API transaction.

How to integrate Splunk with SAP APIM?
Prerequisites
Splunk URL: https://{{HostName}}:{{Port}}/services/collector/event
Authorization header: Splunk {{Token}}
Data format: JSON
{
    "index": "test_sap",
    "host": "cpidev",
    "sourcetype": "sap:api:events",
    "source": "API00XX",
    "event": {
        "message": "Event Data/Structure Here"
    }
}
Solution Overview

The implementation uses SAP APIM policies and JavaScript to build a structured log payload and forward it to Splunk. PreFlow handles authentication-related failures before the request reaches the backend, while PostFlow captures response status, payload details, and timing metrics after target processing. This separation is useful because it ensures that both failed and completed transactions can be logged based on configured conditions. It also gives the team flexibility to log only exceptions or all traffic, depending on operational needs and expected log volume.

Policy Steps: PreFlow
KVM: SplunkDetails (Connection Settings)

This policy reads the Splunk connection details from a Key Value Map, including the URL, resource path, host, index, source type, and proxy-specific success logging flag. KVM is used here to externalize configuration so that endpoint changes can be handled without modifying policy definitions or JavaScript logic. If any required KVM entry is missing at runtime, the downstream service callout may fail or produce incomplete log events, so these values should be validated carefully in each environment.

<KeyValueMapOperations mapIdentifier="SplunkDetails" async="true" continueOnError="false" enabled="true"
    xmlns="http://www.sap.com/apimgmt">
    <!-- PUT stores the key value pair mentioned inside the element -->
    <Get assignTo="private.splunkURL">
        <Key>
            <Parameter>splunkURL</Parameter>
        </Key>
    </Get>
    <Get assignTo="private.splunkResourcePath">
        <Key>
            <Parameter>splunkResourcePath</Parameter>
        </Key>
    </Get>
    <Get assignTo="private.splunkHost">
        <Key>
            <Parameter>splunkHost</Parameter>
        </Key>
    </Get>
    <Get assignTo="private.splunkIndex">
        <Key>
            <Parameter>splunkIndex</Parameter>
        </Key>
    </Get>
    <Get assignTo="private.splunkSourceType">
        <Key>
            <Parameter>splunkSourceType</Parameter>
        </Key>
    </Get>
    <Get assignTo="private.sendSucessToSplunk">
        <Key>
            <Parameter ref="apiproxy.name" />
        </Key>
    </Get>
    <!-- the scope of the key value map. Valid values are environment, organization, apiproxy and policy -->
</KeyValueMapOperations>
KVM: Passwords (Token)

This policy retrieves the Splunk token from a secure Key Value Map entry. Storing the token outside the policy definition helps reduce hardcoding of credentials and makes token rotation easier to manage. If the token is invalid or missing, the call to Splunk will not be authorized, so this is one of the first places to check during troubleshooting.

<KeyValueMapOperations mapIdentifier="Passwords" async="true" continueOnError="false" enabled="true"
    xmlns="http://www.sap.com/apimgmt">
    <!-- PUT stores the key value pair mentioned inside the element -->
    <Get assignTo="private.splunkToken">
        <Key>
            <Parameter>SPLUNKLOGGING_TOKEN</Parameter>
        </Key>
    </Get>
    <!-- the scope of the key value map. Valid values are environment, organization, apiproxy and policy -->
</KeyValueMapOperations>

JavaScript

This JavaScript policy constructs the structured log payload for authentication failures detected in PreFlow. It captures request metadata such as API key, product name, proxy name, environment, client IP address, timestamps, request details, and the validation fault name. This step is important because authentication failures occur before the backend is invoked, so if they are not logged in PreFlow, they may be missed entirely in transaction monitoring.

var logdata = {
    apiKey: context.getVariable("request.header.APIKey"),
    productName: context.getVariable("apiproduct.name"),
    proxyName: context.getVariable("apiproxy.name"),
    environmentName: context.getVariable("environment.name"),
    IpAddress: context.getVariable("client.ip"),
    currentSystemTime: context.getVariable("system.time"),
    currentSystemTimeStamp: context.getVariable("system.timestamp"),
    requestReceivedStartTime: context.getVariable("client.received.start.time"),
    requestReceivedStartTimeStamp: context.getVariable("client.received.start.timestamp"),
    requestReceivedEndTime: context.getVariable("client.received.end.time"),
    requestReceivedEndtTimeStamp: context.getVariable("client.received.end.timestamp"),
    responseCode: "401",
    messageId: context.getVariable("messageid"),
    requestURI: context.getVariable("request.uri"),
    requestVerb: context.getVariable("request.verb"),
    requestContent: context.getVariable("request.content"),
    responseMessage: context.getVariable("oauthV2.VAK-ValidateAPIKey.fault.name")
}
context.setVariable("sapapim.logmessage", JSON.stringify(logdata));

Note : Regarding the variables use the reference SAP Help Link variable-references

Service Callout

This service callout sends the PreFlow log payload to the Splunk HTTP Event Collector endpoint. It is configured asynchronously so that the logging call does not unnecessarily delay the client-facing transaction. The condition should be set so that it runs only when API key or OAuth validation fails; otherwise, Splunk will receive unnecessary events and the logs will become noisy.

Condition: Use oauthV2.VAK-ValidateAPIKey.failed is true for source-side API key authentication. If OAuth-based authentication is used, replace this with oauthV2.failed is true. This condition ensures that only requests with authentication failures are sent to Splunk, which helps keep the logs focused on exception scenarios.

<ServiceCallout async="true" continueOnError="true" enabled="true" xmlns="http://www.sap.com/apimgmt">
    <Request>
        <Set>
            <Headers>
                <Header name="Content-Type">application/json</Header>
                <Header name="Authorization">Splunk {private.splunkToken}</Header>
            </Headers>
            <Payload contentType="application/json" variablePrefix="#" variableSuffix="@">{
                "time": #client.received.start.timestamp@,
                "index": "#private.splunkIndex@",
                "host": "#private.splunkHost@",
                "sourcetype": "#private.splunkSourceType@",
                "source": "#apiproxy.name@",
                "event": {
                "Message": #sapapim.logmessage@
                }
                }</Payload>
            <Verb>POST</Verb>
        </Set>
    </Request>
    <Timeout>30000</Timeout>
    <HTTPTargetConnection>
        <URL>https://{private.splunkURL}/{private.splunkResourcePath}</URL>
    </HTTPTargetConnection>
</ServiceCallout>
Raise Fault

This policy returns a controlled error response to the client when authentication fails. It ensures that the consumer receives a consistent 401 Unauthorized response rather than an inconsistent backend or policy error. This is useful not only for client handling, but also because it keeps the error path predictable while the failure is logged separately to Splunk.

Condition: Use oauthV2.VAK-ValidateAPIKey.failed is true for source-side API key authentication. If OAuth-based authentication is used instead, replace this with oauthV2.failed is true and update the error reference in the policy to oauthV2.OA-VerifyAccessToken.fault.name.

This condition determines whether the exception flow should be triggered when authentication fails. It ensures that only failed authentication requests are handled by the fault policy, which helps maintain consistent error handling and supports accurate logging for monitoring and troubleshooting.

<!-- can be used to create custom messages in case of an error condition -->
<RaiseFault async="true" continueOnError="false" enabled="true" xmlns="http://www.sap.com/apimgmt">
    <!-- Defines the response message returned to the requesting client -->
    <FaultResponse>
        <Set>
            <!-- Sets or overwrites HTTP headers in the respone message -->
            <Headers />
            <Payload contentType="application/json">
                {

                "Error": "{oauthV2.VAK-ValidateAPIKey.fault.name}"

                }
            </Payload>
            <StatusCode>401</StatusCode>
            <!-- sets the reason phrase of the response -->
            <ReasonPhrase>Unauthorized</ReasonPhrase>
        </Set>
    </FaultResponse>
    <IgnoreUnresolvedVariables>true</IgnoreUnresolvedVariables>
</RaiseFault>

Policy Steps: PostFlow
JavaScript

This JavaScript policy prepares the structured log payload after target processing is completed. In addition to request and response details, it captures timing information such as target response time and overall API response time, which makes the logs useful for both troubleshooting and performance analysis. This policy also reads the success logging flag so that the proxy can be configured to send only failures or both failures and successful transactions.

var sendSuccessToSplunk = context.getVariable("private.sendSucessToSplunk");
var logdata = {
    apiKey: context.getVariable("request.header.APIKey"),
    productName: context.getVariable("apiproduct.name"),
    proxyName: context.getVariable("apiproxy.name"),
    environmentName: context.getVariable("environment.name"),
    IpAddress: context.getVariable("client.ip"),
    currentSystemTime: context.getVariable("system.time"),
    currentSystemTimeStamp: context.getVariable("system.timestamp"),
    requestReceivedStartTime: context.getVariable("client.received.start.time"),
    requestReceivedStartTimeStamp: context.getVariable("client.received.start.timestamp"),
    requestReceivedEndTime: context.getVariable("client.received.end.time"),
    requestReceivedEndtTimeStamp: context.getVariable("client.received.end.timestamp"),
    targetResponseTime: context.getVariable("target.received.end.timestamp") - context.getVariable("target.sent.start.timestamp"),
    apiResponseTime: context.getVariable("system.timestamp") - context.getVariable("client.received.end.timestamp"),
    targetResponseEndTime: context.getVariable("target.received.end.time"),
    messageStatusCode: context.getVariable("message.status.code"),
    messageId: context.getVariable("messageid"),
    requestContent: context.getVariable("request.content"),
    requestURI: context.getVariable("request.uri"),
    requestVerb: context.getVariable("request.verb"),
    requestURL: context.getVariable("request.url"),
    responseMessage: context.getVariable("response.content"),
    sendSuccessToSplunk: context.getVariable("private.sendSucessToSplunk")
}
context.setVariable("sapapim.logmessage", JSON.stringify(logdata));
Service Callout

This service callout sends the PostFlow event to Splunk after the response is generated. The condition allows logging for 4xx and 5xx responses by default, and it can also log successful responses when the proxy-specific KVM setting is enabled. This design gives good operational flexibility, because teams that want lower log volume can capture only failures, while teams that need fuller observability can enable success logging.

Condition: Use ((message.status.code =| 4 or message.status.code =| 5) and private.sendSucessToSplunk is false) or private.sendSucessToSplunk is true.

This condition determines whether the transaction should be forwarded to Splunk after response processing. If success logging is disabled, only error responses in the 4xx or 5xx range are sent to Splunk. If success logging is enabled, both successful and failed transactions are logged.

The ProxyName entry in the SplunkDetails KVM controls this behavior. If the value is true, Splunk receives both success and failure messages. If the value is false, only failure messages are sent, which helps reduce log volume while still preserving visibility into exception scenarios.

<ServiceCallout async="true" continueOnError="true" enabled="true" xmlns="http://www.sap.com/apimgmt">
    <Request>
        <Set>
            <Headers>
                <Header name="Content-Type">application/json</Header>
                <Header name="Authorization">Splunk {private.splunkToken}</Header>
            </Headers>
            <Payload contentType="application/json" variablePrefix="#" variableSuffix="@">{
                "time": #client.received.start.timestamp@,
                "index": "#private.splunkIndex@",
                "host": "#private.splunkHost@",
                "sourcetype": "#private.splunkSourceType@",
                "source": "#apiproxy.name@",
                "event": {
                "Message": #sapapim.logmessage@
                }
                }</Payload>
            <Verb>POST</Verb>
        </Set>
    </Request>
    <Timeout>30000</Timeout>
    <HTTPTargetConnection>
        <URL>https://{private.splunkURL}/{private.splunkResourcePath}</URL>
    </HTTPTargetConnection>
</ServiceCallout>
Raise Fault

This policy raises a fault for error responses returned from the target system. It preserves the backend status code and response content so that consumers receive a meaningful error while the same transaction is also available in Splunk for support analysis. Care should be taken when using this policy to ensure that sensitive backend payload content is not exposed to clients or written to logs without review.

Condition: Use (message.status.code =| 4 or message.status.code =| 5).

This condition checks whether the response belongs to an error category, specifically HTTP 4xx or 5xx status codes. It ensures that the policy is triggered only for error responses, which makes it useful for exception handling and error-specific logging.

<!-- can be used to create custom messages in case of an error condition -->
<RaiseFault async="true" continueOnError="false" enabled="true" xmlns="http://www.sap.com/apimgmt">
    <!-- Defines the response message returned to the requesting client -->
    <FaultResponse>
        <Set>
            <!-- Sets or overwrites HTTP headers in the respone message -->
            <Headers />
            <Payload contentType="application/xml">
                {response.content}
            </Payload>
            <StatusCode>{target.response.status.code}</StatusCode>
            <!-- sets the reason phrase of the response -->
            <!--<ReasonPhrase>Bad Request</ReasonPhrase>-->
        </Set>
    </FaultResponse>
    <IgnoreUnresolvedVariables>true</IgnoreUnresolvedVariables>
</RaiseFault>
Logging Behavior

The design supports two logging modes. If success logging is enabled for a proxy, both successful and failed API transactions are sent to Splunk. If success logging is disabled, only error scenarios such as authentication failures and 4xx/5xx responses are logged, which helps reduce logging volume while still preserving operational visibility for exceptions.

Conclusion

This solution enables SAP APIM transaction logging to Splunk for better monitoring of API errors, responses, and performance. By using KVM for secure configuration and logging key events in PreFlow and PostFlow, it gives the integration team flexible control over whether to capture only errors or all transactions in Splunk.

Limitations 

One limitation of this approach is that the success payload cannot be logged when streaming is enabled. This should be considered during design if payload-level success logging is a monitoring requirement.

entry.title: SAP Sapphire 2026: Unlocking Next-Level Innovation to AI with SAP Business AI Platform
entry.content:
*Updated on May 15 with SAP Business AI Platform

Hello SAP Community!

Mark your calendars – SAP Sapphire 2026 is here, and it's set to redefine what's possible. This year, the conversation isn’t just about digital transformation. The focus is on mastering your platform journey to drive continuous innovation, achieve measurable business outcomes, and on how organizations should leverage artificial intelligence. It’s going to be a fantastic opportunity to learn, share together, and of course, I’m especially looking forward to celebrating with everyone at the afterparty. I just found out there’s hazelnut chocolate fruit waffles at Magic Kingdom, so I’m extra excited!

From AI-powered development and intelligent automation to seamless integration and strategic platform management, SAP’s Business AI platform (SAP BAIP) stands as the essential foundation. Its unique differentiation lies in deeply embedding AI across your operations while preserving the invaluable data and business application context, thereby turning challenges into tangible opportunities. Sapphire 2026 will be the pivotal event to launch SAP’s strategic vision, highlighting how AI innovations are integral to the enterprises of tomorrow.

 

The Mandate: Connecting Everything, Across the Entire Landscape

In an era when disconnected landscapes continue to hinder progress, SAP’s customers have consistently shown that the platform's true power lies in end-to-end connectivity. Whether it's your CHRO leveraging SAP SuccessFactors employee data with procurement data for newly onboarding employees, or your CFO optimizing financials with SAP S/4HANA together with external third-party benchmark data, or your CPO driving procurement excellence by keeping warehouse data up to date from supply chain partners, the challenge remains: how do you connect these vital functions, including between the AI agents, to move beyond implementation to true business transformation?

This year, you’ll see how SAP Business AI platform serves as the critical foundation for SAP’s business applications, making this connectivity a reality. It's designed to streamline operations, foster innovation, and enable real-time analysis of insights across your entire business landscape.

 

Spotlight on Transformation: Breakthrough Use Cases for a Connected Future

This Sapphire, we're particularly excited to unveil new use cases that exemplify the SAP Business AI platform’s power to address today's most pressing business challenges.

 

1. Integrate HR data and processes with government and third‑party systems

  • The Challenge: Navigating complex and evolving HR regulations, increasing demands for efficiency in HR service delivery, and managing diverse workforces effectively.
  • How the SAP BAIP helps: By integrating HR data in SAP SuccessFactors (or elsewhere) and processes with government and third-party systems through SAP Integration Suite, organizations can ensure compliance with global and local regulations, automate processes, and streamline interactions with third-parties. This connected process frees the HR department from administrative burdens, creating streamlined employee experiences and compliant data.
  • Recommended sessions at Sapphire:
    • Proving the business value of standardizing on SAP Integration Suite | BTP2350: Hear how teams quantify business value after standardizing on SAP Integration Suite: before-and-after metrics, faster time to integrate, fewer incidents, lower operating effort, and readiness for a mixed SAP and third-party software landscape. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • PPG Industries: Building an AI-ready, clean core integration foundation | BTP2638: See how APIs, events, and cloud integration reduce complexity, improve visibility, and establish an AI-ready foundation for future innovation across SAP and third-party systems. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Building a foundation for AI with an integrated core HR system | HCM1742: Learn how a connected approach to core HR, payroll, workforce management, and HR service delivery can help you streamline your people and business processes while enabling AI readiness. 
      🗓️ADD NOW

2. Drive profitable growth with secure, personalized digital experiences

  • The Challenge: Organizations struggle with complex processes and fragmented access to customer data, facing increasing competition and rising customer expectations. Data silos often lead to slow and reactive decision-making, impacting customer satisfaction and growth.
  • How SAP BAIP helps: SAP Customer Identity and Access Management (SAP CIAM) protects sensitive customer data and meets global compliance requirements. By consolidating and integrating customer data across various systems with SAP’s platform, it enables the creation of secure, personalized digital experiences that overcome data silos. This supports dynamic and tailored customer interactions, enhancing customer acquisition and retention.
  • Recommended sessions at Sapphire:
    • Standardize, secure, and scale with core platform capabilities | BTP1315: Learn how application lifecycle management, security, and interoperability unlock SAP’s Platform to drive agility and growth. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Invisible security: SAP Customer Identity and Access Management | BTP1316: Discover how SAP Customer Identity and Access Management accelerates your business by harnessing AI-assisted risk analysis to stop threats in real time, protecting customers behind the scenes without disrupting experiences. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Secure by design: How SAP is redefining agent execution | BTP2641: Learn how SAP is developing the secure execution layer, combining runtime containment with the governance of AI-enabled data fabric solutions from SAP to define production-ready agentic AI.
      🗓️🗓ADD NOW

 

3. Leverage AI Capabilities in ABAP Cloud Development

  • The Challenge: Developers struggle to balance rapid delivery with maintaining code standards, while traditional coding, debugging, and testing consume a significant portion of their time. Complex legacy code also slows productivity.
  • How SAP BAIP helps: Leveraging AI capabilities directly within ABAP Cloud Development, using tools like SAP Joule for Developers, can dramatically increase developer productivity. AI assists with code generation, ensures code quality, and helps your development teams understand complex systems, freeing them to focus on innovation.
  • Recommended sessions at Sapphire:
    • Agentic ABAP with SAP Joule for Developers: Build faster, migrate smarter | JOU1428: Find out how AI agents with ABAP simplify migration, speed up coding, and increase productivity, even in older versions of SAP S/4HANA. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • ABAP Cloud: Entering the new era of ABAP development | BTP2107: The ABAP Cloud development model, agentic development experience in VS Code, the SAP Joule for Developers add-on, and agentic custom code migration join forces for the next era of growth in ABAP development tools. 
      🗓️ADD NOW
    • Introduction to ABAP Cloud supported by generative AI | BTP1429: Learn what ABAP Cloud is, get insights into the latest technology advancements, and see how generative AI boosts your adoption of ABAP Cloud. 
      🗓️ADD NOW

 

4. Modernize On-Premise Integration to Cloud with PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite Migration

  • The Challenge: Organizations still using SAP Process Integration/Process Orchestration (SAP PI/PO) are facing the impending need to modernize the integration methods. With a complex integration landscape comprising multiple integration solutions, not only does it add complexity, but it also creates greater technical debt.

entry.title: Automate log level commands - SAP CPI
entry.content:
Introduction:

The interface is designed to run manually, timer-based, and through external systems.

API resource we used: /Operations/com.sap.it.op.tmn.commands.dashboard.webui.IntegrationComponentSetMplLogLevelCommand

Overview:

Managing log levels for SAP Cloud Integration artifacts becomes challenging when:

  • Multiple artifacts need log level changes simultaneously
  • Manual operations through "Manage Integration Content" are time-consuming
  • Troubleshooting scenarios require quick log level adjustments across related flows
  • Environment migrations need consistent log level configurations
  • Incident response demands rapid enabling of DEBUG/TRACE for multiple components

Limitations of manual process:

  • Navigate to each integration flow individually
  • Click through UI for each artifact
  • Risk of missing artifacts
  • No audit trail of changes
  • Time-consuming for 10+ artifacts

Common Scenarios when this can be helpful:

  1. Incident Response: Enable DEBUG/TRACE for all flows in a specific integration package
  2. Environment Migration: Apply consistent log levels across ENT/DEV/PROD
  3. Post-Deployment: Reset all flows to INFO after troubleshooting
  4. Scheduled Maintenance: Temporarily enable verbose logging during testing windows
  5. Package-Level Management: Set log levels for all flows in a business domain (e.g., all "Sales Order_*" flows)

In short: Use the API programmatically to perform bulk operations efficiently.

Log levels we majorly use:

Level

Description

When to Use

Performance Impact

NONE

No logging

Production (when monitoring not needed)

Minimal

INFO

Basic execution info

Standard production monitoring

Low

DEBUG

Detailed execution steps

Troubleshooting, development

Medium

TRACE

Full message payload & headers

Deep debugging, payload inspection

High

Integration flow design:

 

  • To run the interface both from cpi and external call (e.g. postman), we have added two main integration processes.
  • As we are passing a simple and formal payload hence converter is used in the first place after which the process call executes the local integration process.
  • In the local integration process, we are splitting the I flow ids if passed more than 1 in input.
  • After getting the I flow id, the same can be used to form the required payload which goes to the final local integration process through the process call.
  • We have configured 3 different receivers to differentiate the tenants i.e. ENT/DEV/PROD.
  • All the dynamic values are externalized nd configured in the runtime without explicitly modifying the interface.

Configuration example of http protocol.

  • We have created OAUTH credential using the token URL, Api key, Api secret from the specific tenant’s service key on BTP and the credential for all three environments i.e. ENT, DEV and PROD.
  • As the credentials are deployed in the security material on ENT tenant itself hence interface transport to the above environment is not needed.

Request example:

Header value to be passed for selecting the tenant:

Body value to be passed:

<IFLOWID>,<LOGLEVEL>

Response example:

logLevel,traceActive,traceEnabled,traceExpiresAt,iflowID

TRACE,true,true,Mon Apr 06 08:03:40 UTC 2026,BRAVO_Delivery_FSAP

ERROR,NA,true,NA,MDGPLM_MasterInspectionCharacteristics_GSAP

The response is in csv and to make it more readable we can open it in excel and the final response will look like this:

logLevel

traceActive

traceEnabled

traceExpiresAt

iflowID

TRACE

true

TRUE

Mon Apr 06 08:01:39 UTC 2026

BRAVO_Delivery_FSAP

ERROR

NA

TRUE

NA

MDGPLM_MasterInspectionCharacteristics_GSAP

Conclusion: 

The manual efforts can be reduced if it's used properly and is helpful majorly during the monitoring and troubleshooting where many of the interfaces in the same package can be put on an specific log level without explicitly touching the integration system again and again.

Important: Higher log levels (DEBUG, TRACE) generate more data and can impact system performance and storage. Use them temporarily for troubleshooting and revert to INFO or NONE for production.

Hope it helps someone someday, happy integrating 😊.

entry.title: Batch File Pickup and Dynamic ZIP Creation in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Picking Multiple Files and Creating ZIP Files in SAP Cloud Integration — A Complete Guide

Introduction  

While developing integrations in SAP Cloud Integration, file handling is one of the most common tasks encountered, and in modern integration scenarios, organizations deal with a large number of files arriving on the SFTP server that must be processed and sent to another third-party system. So, dealing with a large number of files at once can impact the system. To address this, files can be processed in batches so that additional operations can be performed and the interface runs smoothly.

This blog explains how batch file processing and ZIP file creation can be implemented in SAP Integration Suite to handle large volume of files efficiently.

Requirements:  

  • Pick 10 files from the source SFTP server
  • Combine the files into a ZIP archive and post it to another SFTP server

Condition: When the ZIP file is extracted, the original file names must remain unchanged  

This approach is especially useful for controlled batch processing, scheduled integrations, and cases that require dynamic file compression.  

 

Prerequisites — Server and Folder Setup  

Before getting started with the integration flow, ensure that the dedicated SFTP server is configured and the directories are created as shown below. 

Create the following directories on the server:  

  • inbound — Source directory where incoming files are placed   
  • tempstore — Temporary directory used to store appended batch data   
  • outbound — Target directory where final ZIP files are delivered

 

Step-by-Step Implementation  

Step 1 — Start by configuring the SFTP sender adapter to control the number of files processed during each run.  

Key Configuration  

Max Messages Per Poll = 10  (in this scenario) 

This ensures that only 10 files are picked in a single batch.  

 

 

Important Tip  

Using a scheduler with a controlled time interval is recommended.

The batch condition logic might not function as intended if the polling interval is set too frequently (for instance, every 10 seconds). Files may end up being processed individually rather than collectively.

Longer scheduling intervals guarantee that all necessary files are available before processing starts. 

In this scenario, 10 files are processed per batch. If more than 10 files are involved, a recurring condition (for example, “Every 5 min”) or even the Advanced Scheduler can be used to ensure that files are grouped and processed in consistent batches.

 

Step 2 — Store File Metadata Using Content Modifier  

After picking the files, the next step is to preserve both the filename and file content. This is necessary because once files are aggregated, their original metadata may be lost.  

Payload Structure:  

<payload>  
    <filename>${header.CamelFileName}</filename>  
    <body>  
        ${in.body}  
    </body>  
</payload>

This ensures:  

  • Filename is preserved  
  • File content is preserved  
  • Data can be safely aggregated and split later

 

Step 3 — Append All Files into a Single Storage File  

The Send step is used to write all payloads into a single file on a temporary SFTP location.  

This step uses Append Mode.  

Key Configuration  

 Append Mode = Enabled  

 

 

Step 4 — Detect Batch Completion Using Router  

A Router is used to determine whether all files in the batch have been processed.  

The router checks the following condition:  

${property.CamelBatchComplete} = 'true'

What This Means  

  • The property becomes true only after all files in the batch are processed  
  • Processing continues only when the batch is complete  
  • If not complete, the flow ends 

 

 

Step 5 — Read the Combined Data Using Poll Enrich  

Once the batch is complete, the system must retrieve the aggregated file.  

This is done using a Poll Enrich step.  

 

Note

"Before Poll Enrich, the message contains the last processed file, after using the Replace aggregation algorithm, the message is replaced with the combined batch file retrieved from temporary storage."  

The SFTP adapter should be configured accordingly. 

 

 

Step 6 — Add a Root Tag to the Combined Payload  

After retrieving the combined data, a Content Modifier is used to wrap the entire payload inside a root element.  

Payload Structure  

<data>  
${in.body}  
</data> 

Why is this required? 

  • To process the XML a single root element is required and without this, it may be difficult for the splitter to perform the split operation as the structure becomes invalid. 

 

Step 7 — Split the Combined Data into Individual Payloads  

A General Splitter is used to split the entire payload into individual messages. 

 

Step 8 — Extract Filename and Body Using Content Modifier  

With the help of this Content Modifier step, the file name and the data can be extracted from each payload (individual split message). 

Properties Created  

FileName  
DataBody  

Message Body Configuration  

${property.DataBody}  

This camel expression is used to call the file content into the message body of content modifier. 

 

 

Step 9 — Create ZIP Files Using Gather Step  

The Gather step is used to create ZIP file dynamically.  

Key Configuration  

  • Incoming Format = Any  
  • Aggregation Algorithm = ZIP  
  • File Name = ${property.FileName}  

Purpose  

  • Dynamically compress the files 
  • Original file names are preserved 

 

Step 10 — Send ZIP Files to Target SFTP Server  

This is the final step where the created ZIP file is sent to the receiver system. 

 

Scheduling Consideration (Critical) {Refer Step 1}  

The scheduling of the integration flow must be time-specific instead of running it continuously. 

Recommendation~ 

Set the scheduler in such as:  

  • At a fixed time on daily basis
  • Hourly basis 
  • At a particular event  

Why is this required? 

If the integration flow runs too frequently:

  • Files may be processed individually 
  • Files may not be picked in proper batches 
  • Aggregation logic may fail 

Scheduling ensures batch processing is controlled and efficient. 

Output / Result  

After deploying the integration flow, the system successfully processes files in controlled batches and generates a compressed output file.  

Observed Behavior  

  • 10 files are picked in a batch from the inbound folder 
  • A ZIP file is created using the Gather palette element  
  • ZIP filename: Batch20260425120549.zip  
  • Zip file is posted to receiver system 
  • When ZIP file is unzipped original file names are preserved

 

 Conclusion  

This blog post covered how to pick multiple files from SFTP using batch processing, combine them into a zip file using gather and post to another SFTP server, preserving file content and file names. 

I'd appreciate any insights, improvements or feedback on this approach. 

If you have questions about the configuration steps or want to share your experiences, please feel free to leave a comment below. 

Happy Integrating!!!

entry.title: Automating MPL and Payload Retrieval in SAP Integration Suite for Enhanced Monitoring
entry.content:
Why we build this solution,

The primary objective is to design a centralized and automated Monitoring solution capable of dynamically retrieving MPL Logs, extracting payload attachment and provide a reusable framework for alerting through Mails or other channel for Integration support activities.

If there is a specific requirement to identify the failed interfaces with Status Message, its Payloads with the exact error information across the Multiple packages within in the required Date and Time range. That required navigation through several monitoring. Now we can achieve this without going to the Monitoring page.

Introduction

Monitoring and troubleshooting integration flows in SAP Integration Suite can become complex when dealing with multiple packages and interfaces. Accessing Message processing Logs (MPL) and their Payload attachments is essential for debugging, Auditing, and Reporting.

This Blog demonstrates a fully Automated Approach to:

  • Fetch the MPL logs dynamically
  • Filter logs based on Time range and integration flow Name 
  • Retrieve payload attachments
  • Process and store Payload data

Step 1: Create OAuth2 client Credentials

Navigate to Monitor→ Integration and APIs→ Manage Security→ Security Material 

create a new credential of type OAuth 2 client credentials
Maintain:

  • Client Id
  • Client secret
  • Token Service URL

OAuth2 client credentials to securely connect to the SAP APIs.

Step 2: Implementation in Sap Integration Suite

Design an Integration Flow by using a Timer Trigger and then configure all the necessary content Modifier, this is where we need to set the control filtering Parameters for MPL Logs. 

Step 3: configure Http Receiver (MPL ODATA  API)

Configure HTTP Adapter:

  • Method:  GET
  • URL:
  • https://<tenant>/api/V1/MessageProcessingLogs 
  • Headers: Accept: application/Json

Step 4: Extract Message GUID

Parse the MPL response which we get from the O DATA API Call and extract the Message GUID (MPL_ID) for the further processing.

In the similar way we can configure the HTTP Adapter to call ODATA APIs to retrieve the attachments and its corresponding value. 

During the Implementation we were also able to extract the Failed Interfaces error Information for specific MPL ID and send the error logs for Monitoring.  Can be used for Error handling in the later stage of development.

 

Challenges faced during Implementation

One of the key challenges during the implementation was to handle the dynamic retrieval of payload attachment using SAP Integration OData APIs. Since the attachments, Metadata and the Payloads are retrieved through multiple APIs calls, designing the flow dynamically without hardcoded package name required careful handling of the MPL properties.

Another challenge we came across was managing the Namespace handling and the Splitter configuration while processing the XML response from APIs. Proper XPath handling and dynamically routing logic were essential to ensure the reliable payload extraction. And throughout the Designing and Execution of this particular Integration flow I have gained a deeper Learning and insight into SAP Integration suite Monitoring APIs and Dynamic I flow filtering.

 

Conclusion:

This Approach demonstrate how SAP Integration Suite Monitoring capabilities can be significantly Enhanced using automated MPL and payload retrieving mechanism to build a Robust monitoring solution in SAP Integration Suite, this can be further Enhanced to 

  • Integrate with Monitoring Dashboards
  • Automated Alert Mechanism detection
  • Dynamically retrieves MPL logs
  • Fetches payload Attachments
  • Supports audit and Analytics

This approach significantly improves visibility, automation, reduces downtime, and enhance operational efficiency in enterprise integrations.

entry.title: Exploring a Desktop Companion for SAP Integration Suite Development
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

Over the last few years working with SAP Integration Suite landscapes, I kept noticing the same pattern during day-to-day integration work: a lot of time is spent switching contexts between tenants, reviewing artifacts manually, documenting integrations, troubleshooting monitor messages, and preparing deployments across environments.

Especially in landscapes with DEV, QA, and PROD tenants, even relatively simple tasks can become repetitive and fragmented. Comparing iFlows between systems, checking whether artifacts are aligned, reviewing failed messages, or generating technical documentation often involves multiple browser tabs, monitor screens, exported files, and manual notes.

Because of that, I started building a local desktop application called SAP IS Companion as a personal side project to explore whether some of these repeated workflows could be simplified into a more focused developer workspace.

The goal is not to replace SAP Integration Suite itself, but to provide a companion-style workbench for inspection, comparison, documentation, migration preparation, monitoring, and developer support tasks around Integration Suite development.

Tenant Comparison and Landscape Visibility

One of the most common pain points I encountered was comparing content across tenants.

When working across DEV, QA, and PROD environments, it can become difficult to quickly identify:

  • Missing packages
  • Missing iFlows
  • Version differences
  • Deployment inconsistencies
  • Landscape drift over time

Today, many teams still rely on manual checks or exported artifacts to validate tenant alignment.

The comparison workflow in SAP IS Companion focuses on helping visualize those differences between tenants in a simpler way before deployments or migration activities occur.
